result.chapter_summary: Educational outcomes and social fairness depend on the 'Shanker Principle,' which mandates specific statewide curriculum frameworks and topic-based assessments. The author argues that the prevailing 'skills-based' approach is a delusion because cognitive science demonstrates that skills are domain-specific rather than general and transferable.

claims.text: States require statewide curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment systems to ensure students and teachers know what content must be learned.
claims.source_passage: What we really need—at the very least—are statewide curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment systems. Then, students and teachers in every school will know what kids are responsible for learning and whether or not they have learned it.

claims.text: Specific statewide curriculum frameworks are the only means by which American grade schools can become high-quality and equitable.
claims.source_passage: They are accurate sentiments, and they are, as far as my knowledge extends, the only means by which our grade schools can become first rate and fair.

claims.text: The educational theory of imparting general skills without a specific curriculum is a 'double delusion.'
claims.source_passage: The alternative theory for universal excellence is to impart non-existent general skills without venturing to require a definite statewide curriculum framework. That’s the fruit of a double delusion.

claims.text: Unspecific content guidelines do not serve any actual guiding purpose for education.
claims.source_passage: First, it’s a delusion to suppose that unspecific content guidelines serve any truly guiding purpose.

claims.text: All-purpose reading-comprehension, critical-thinking, and complexity-managing skills do not exist as transferable entities.
claims.source_passage: And second, it’s a delusion to suppose that there exist all-purpose reading-comprehension skills, and all-purpose critical-thinking skills, and all-purpose complexity-managing skills.

claims.text: Any cognitive psychologist claiming that general, all-purpose skills exist would lose professional respect.
claims.source_passage: But they do not, and any cognitive psychologist who claimed they do would lose reputation and respect from his or her colleagues.

claims.text: Standardized tests drive the educational system but are unproductive unless they are based on specific curriculum topics.
claims.source_passage: Tests drive the system, but unless the tests are based upon the topics of the curriculum, they are inherently unproductive in the early grades.

claims.text: Current standardized tests reward children from advantaged homes rather than testing what has been taught in school.
claims.source_passage: The tests simply reward children from advantaged homes, and do not test what schools have actually taught!

claims.text: Making early reading tests independent of defined school content is unfair to disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: To make early reading tests independent of school content (which they must be if the school content remains undefined) is unfair to disadvantaged students and indifferent to school quality with respect to those students.

claims.text: The Common Core State Standards are evasive and avoid the content specificity required for effective education.
claims.source_passage: Take for example the current Common Core State Standards. They are good examples of the evasiveness and fear of the inevitable criticism that would certainly come initially with the specificity that Shanker calls for.

claims.text: Human skills and expertise are domain-specific and generally not transferable from one area to another.
claims.source_passage: The technical literature on skills is clear: being expert in one domain does not make you an expert in another. Expertise is area-specific; skill and expertise are domain specific to use the technical term. Acquired human skills are not, by and large, transferable.

claims.text: Disadvantaged children are unable to catch up academically when schools fail to provide specific, required knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Disadvantaged children are unable to catch up when our schools do not provide the specific knowledge needed for the specific tasks at hand.

claims.text: The term 'complexity' as used in the Common Core standards lacks scientific validity because text-complexity is dependent upon the reader's familiarity with the subject.
claims.source_passage: I’m particularly depressed by the tossing around of the term “complexity” in the Common Core standards. This term has zero scientific validity, because text-complexity is dependent upon familiarity, which varies from person to person.

claims.text: Measures of 'readability' have been proven to be nonsensical since the early 1980s.
claims.source_passage: Answer: because measures of “readability” have been proved nonsense since the early 1980s.

claims.text: The technical literature demonstrates the domain-specificity of skills, meaning skills depend on specific knowledge for specific tasks.
claims.source_passage: The technical literature is clear on “the domain-specificity of skills.” The principle holds for pretty much every skill, not just verbal comprehension.

claims.text: Educational theorists use terminological sleight of hand, such as substituting 'complexity' for 'readability,' to avoid curriculum specificity.
claims.source_passage: This maneuver to avoid curriculum specificity is similar in its terminological sleight of hand to the various terms used to describe “constructivism”

claims.text: Constructivism, or the idea that children learn best by constructing their own knowledge through projects, is empirically ineffective.
claims.source_passage: When an experiment shows that it doesn’t work, we don’t give up the natural-development, learning-by-doing idea, we stop calling it “constructivism” and we call it “discovery learning.”

claims.text: The developmental-project-method tradition is so dominant in teacher-training faculties that failed methods are simply renamed rather than abandoned.
claims.source_passage: When those get debunked, still newer terms will arise – so powerful is the developmental-project-method tradition in our teacher-training faculties. But the method, despite its persistent promotion, does not work.

claims.text: American elementary education is currently based on two incorrect theories: that project-based learning is effective and that general reading/thinking skills exist.
claims.source_passage: American elementary education is thus based currently upon two incorrect theories: namely that project-based learning is effective, and that general skills of reading comprehension and critical-thinking exist.

claims.text: The fallacious assumptions of project-based learning and general skills are sufficient to explain the mediocre outcomes of American elementary education.
claims.source_passage: These two fallacious assumptions are alone enough to explain the mediocre outcomes of American elementary education.

claims.text: Legislators avoid specific curriculum content decisions by framing them as a defense against tyranny or dictatorship.
claims.source_passage: Decisions about content are of course contentious. They get avoided by legislators on noble-sounding grounds. “We don’t want the government deciding what our children shall learn. That’s the road to tyranny.”

claims.text: Legislatures should mandate specific topic sequences (e.g., teaching facts about Jefferson) while leaving the interpretive treatment of those topics to teachers.
claims.source_passage: But we’re only asking our legislatures to decide topic sequences, not how the topics are treated. You can say Jefferson was a hero or you can say he was a hypocrite, but legislatures need to say: you will teach about Jefferson in Grade X

claims.text: Current public education policy discussions focus on structures like funding or charters rather than essential content.
claims.source_passage: To avoid concrete curriculum frameworks at the state level, our public discussions of education policy are currently about structures not content... The discussions are chiefly about school funding or about whether charter schools should be encouraged.

claims.text: The question 'Who decides?' is a conversation stopper used to cloak educational irresponsibility.
claims.source_passage: The current conversation stopper about the Shanker principle is to respond with the phrase “Who decides?” That challenge now greets concrete proposals, thus cloaking educational irresponsibility in the mantle of virtue.

claims.text: States have abandoned their constitutional responsibility for education by assuming that abstract skills exist instead of setting concrete curriculum frameworks.
claims.source_passage: Under the Constitution the states do. They have abandoned their responsibility by assuming that abstract “reading comprehension skills” and abstract “critical thinking skills” exist.

claims.text: Topic-concreteness with shared-sequence of topics is the only way to achieve universal literacy.
claims.source_passage: Topic-concreteness with shared-sequence of topics is the only avenue to making every child literate.

claims.text: Specific knowledge and wide vocabulary make a person 'operationally smarter' by enabling rapid understanding of new information.
claims.source_passage: You can better understand what’s going on around you and can also understand new things rapidly and effectively. It makes you operationally smarter.

claims.text: Higher student income correlates with both specific vocational knowledge learned early and wide general knowledge.
claims.source_passage: A recent long-term study in Spain showed that higher income of students arises from two principles: the more specific knowledge you learn early about a specific vocation plus the wide general knowledge

claims.text: Shared knowledge leads to a more competent and unified citizenry, which is of high importance in a democracy.
claims.source_passage: It [shared knowledge] leads to a more competent and unified citizenry (of high importance in a democracy).

claims.text: Shared knowledge leads to greater equality of competence and of income.
claims.source_passage: It also leads to greater equality of competence and of income.

claims.text: Teen-age reading scores predict adult income because wider knowledge and effective vocabulary allow for more effective communication and learning.
claims.source_passage: Teen-age reading scores predict adult income because the wider the knowledge you have, and the more effective the vocab­ulary you have – then the more effectively you can communicate and the more effectively you can learn.

claims.text: Possessing wider knowledge and vocabulary makes an individual operationally smarter by allowing them to understand new things rapidly.
claims.source_passage: You can better understand what’s going on around you and can also understand new things rapidly and effectively. It makes you operationally smarter.

claims.text: The American elementary classroom is inherently unequal because the vocabulary size of incoming students is hugely varied.
claims.source_passage: At first the American elementary classroom is inherently unequal because the vocabulary size of incoming students is hugely varied.

claims.text: Under current educational arrangements, the initial inequality in student vocabulary is rarely overcome.
claims.source_passage: Under current arrangements, that initial inequality is rarely overcome!

claims.text: Initial vocabulary inequality is consistently overcome in shared knowledge schools.
claims.source_passage: But it is always overcome in shared knowledge schools, where each new class uses language based on knowledge and language from already studied subject matters.

claims.text: Shared knowledge in the classroom builds a language community that overcomes class-based differences.
claims.source_passage: That builds up a language community in the classroom. Class-based difference is therefore overcome under the Shanker principle, which has the effect of equalizing student vocabulary for the classroom topic at hand.

claims.text: Physical inclusivity in schools is worthless or even harmful without linguistic and mental inclusiveness.
claims.source_passage: Yet physical inclusiveness is worthless or even harmful without linguistic inclusiveness.

claims.text: Children require a grade-specific 'ethnicity' (shared background knowledge) to understand classroom instruction and make progress.
claims.source_passage: Third graders need third-grade ethnicity so they can understand what is going on and become enabled to make progress along with more advantaged children.

claims.text: Effective classroom materials (examples, analogies, allusions) require the author to know what the child has already learned.
claims.source_passage: If the makers of those materials know what the child has already learned, consider how much more effective will be the examples, analogies, and allusions of the materials.

claims.text: Statewide curriculum frameworks must be specific and detailed enough to determine grade-by-grade topics for publishers to follow.
claims.source_passage: legislators who commit to specific “statewide curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment systems,” that offer curriculum guides specific and detailed enough to determine the chief grade-by-grade topics that the publishers will use in their materials for each grade.

claims.text: Without specificity and commonality in the sequence of topics, there can be no equality in education.
claims.source_passage: For let it be stated as a key principle of elementary education in any nation: without specificity and commonality in the sequence of topics, there can be no equality!

claims.text: The Core Knowledge Foundation provides the specificity needed to exemplify the Shanker principle.
claims.source_passage: Whatever its defects, it offers the specificity needed to exemplify the benefits of the Shanker principle.

claims.text: There can be no educational equality without a specific curriculum.
claims.source_passage: There can be no equality!

claims.text: The Core Knowledge Foundation and its members do not receive personal royalties from the sale of their educational materials.
claims.source_passage: Nobody at the Core Knowledge Foundation personally gets a cent of royalty on anything.

claims.text: The political debate over charter schools involves the claim that they 'cream' the best students rather than reflecting typical school problems.
claims.source_passage: the political debate over charter schools has some people claiming that charters “cream” students and don’t reflect the problems of the regular schools.

claims.text: Lyles-Crouch School represents a typical, non-selective public school with significant demographic challenges.
claims.source_passage: So, here’s a plain vanilla, regular school in Alexandria, Virginia, with plenty of typical problems.

claims.text: Lyles-Crouch School has consistently outperformed other schools in its district for multiple years using a specific knowledge sequence.
claims.source_passage: Over the last three school years, we have outperformed all other schools in the Alexandria City Public Schools.

claims.text: Almost any public school can implement the Core Knowledge Sequence without institutional problems.
claims.source_passage: First of all, it is a telling fact that almost any public school can, without any problem, use that Sequence.

claims.text: Most school systems fail to avoid curricular incoherence because they lack a detailed topic sequence.
claims.source_passage: Answer: the school systems don’t avoid incoherence.

claims.text: The learning objectives in typical district frameworks, such as Alexandria's, are nebulous and non-specific regarding content.
claims.source_passage: Note especially the non-specific content of English language arts... That is the very sort of thing experienced teachers complain of in Chapter 4.

claims.text: Nebulous educational objectives force individual teachers and textbook makers to decide on specific topics themselves, leading to systemic inconsistency.
claims.source_passage: Such nebulous objectives force each teacher (or textbook maker) to decide the specific topics for themselves.

claims.text: A valid curriculum framework must ensure that students and teachers know precisely what knowledge is required for mastery.
claims.source_passage: That would ensure, Shanker said, “that students and teachers in every school will know what kids are responsible for learning and whether or not they have learned it.”

claims.text: Current testing systems are fundamentally unfair because they do not provide enough guidance for schools to prepare disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: That makes our current testing system essentially unfair, and it assures ineffectiveness in overcoming the comprehension gaps between advantaged and disadvantaged students.

claims.text: Only extreme specificity in curriculum can bridge the comprehension gap between advantaged and disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: Here is the kind of specificity that alone can ensure “that students and teachers in every school will know what kids are responsible for learning and whether or not they have learned it.”

claims.text: The Core Knowledge Sequence exemplifies what Al Shanker meant by a functional 'curriculum framework.'
claims.source_passage: By contrast, the Core Knowledge Sequence is what Al Shanker had in mind as a “curriculum framework.”

claims.text: The Alexandria framework fails to provide schools and teachers with enough guidance to prepare disadvantaged students for tests.
claims.source_passage: Does the Alexandria framework offer schools and teachers enough guidance to prepare disadvantaged students to have a fair shot at the upcoming test? No!

claims.text: The current American testing system is essentially unfair because it lacks a corresponding specific curriculum.
claims.source_passage: That makes our current testing system essentially unfair, and it assures ineffectiveness in overcoming the comprehension gaps between advantaged and disadvantaged students.

claims.text: Kindergarten education should include a specific body of traditional literature, including Mother Goose rhymes and international fables.
claims.source_passage: Kindergarten Language Arts: Mother Goose and Other Traditional Poems... Fiction—Stories: “The Bremen Town Musicians” (Brothers Grimm)... “Momotaro: Peach Boy” (Japanese folktale)

claims.text: Shared cultural literacy in the US requires familiarity with specific American folk heroes and tall tales such as Johnny Appleseed and Casey Jones.
claims.source_passage: American Folk Heroes and Tall Tales: Johnny Appleseed, Casey Jones

claims.text: Historical instruction for five-year-olds should include substantive topics like early exploration, the concept of democracy, and the history of slavery.
claims.source_passage: Early Exploration and Settlement... Democracy (rule of the people)... Slavery: some people were not free

claims.text: Visual arts education should move beyond activity to the identification of specific artistic elements and masterworks.
claims.source_passage: Observe the use of color in: Pieter Bruegel, The Hunters in the Snow; Helen Frankenthaler, Blue Atmosphere; Paul Gauguin, Tahitian Landscape; Pablo Picasso, Le Gourmet

claims.text: Specific curriculum frameworks in arts and music, such as those used at Lyles-Crouch, raise the verbal abilities of students to the top of their state.
claims.source_passage: Whatever objections might be raised against this curriculum framework, it’s obvious that it has raised the verbal abilities of its students to the top in its state.

claims.text: There is a massive public demand for Core Knowledge schooling in the South Bronx that currently exceeds capacity by tens of thousands of students.
claims.source_passage: In the South Bronx, 25,000 disappointed kindergartners are turned away from seven Core Knowledge schools every year. That potentially represents some 50,000 disappointed parents every year

claims.text: If educational disadvantage can be overcome in the South Bronx, it can be overcome anywhere in the United States.
claims.source_passage: The South Bronx is the poorest area in New York City. If disadvantage is overcome in the South Bronx, it can be overcome anywhere in the USA.

claims.text: Effective performance in public debate requires mastery of 'public language' and its publicly shared associations, which the author defines as shared ethnicity.
claims.source_passage: To perform effectively requires mastery of the public language with its publicly shared associations and values – shared ethnicity.

claims.text: Core Knowledge schooling enables students to overcome 'home disadvantage.'
claims.source_passage: Their schooling has overcome home disadvantage.

claims.text: Every single eighth grader in the seven South Bronx Core Knowledge schools achieved admission to selective high schools.
claims.source_passage: Every single eighth grader in all seven of the South Bronx Core Knowledge schools proceeded to win admission to a selective high school!

claims.text: The success of South Bronx Core Knowledge students is significantly higher than the citywide average for selective high school admission.
claims.source_passage: Their 100 percent admission to select high schools is especially striking. In the whole of New York City the rate of acceptance of the 28,000 ambitious eighth graders who take the admission test is 14 percent!

claims.text: The success of Core Knowledge students is not the result of selective admission processes or 'creaming' because students are admitted by random lottery.
claims.source_passage: These low-income children in the picture had not been specially selected at an early age for kindergarten admission. On the contrary, they were randomly admitted by blind l

claims.text: Mastering the public language through specific curriculum prepares children to be good citizens and earners.
claims.source_passage: These children are on their way to being good citizens – and good earners.

claims.text: Schooling can successfully overcome home disadvantage in terms of academic outcomes.
claims.source_passage: Their schooling has overcome home disadvantage.

claims.text: The Icahn Core Knowledge schools achieved a 100 percent admission rate to selective high schools for their eighth graders.
claims.source_passage: Every single eighth grader in all seven of the South Bronx Core Knowledge schools proceeded to win admission to a selective high school!

claims.text: The success of students in the Icahn schools was caused by their schooling rather than student selection.
claims.source_passage: It was their schooling – conducted by their devoted teachers under the leadership of Superintendent Jeffrey Litt – that achieved the result.

claims.text: The Icahn Core Knowledge schools operate on the same per-student budget as comparable NYC public schools.
claims.source_passage: It is important to note, however, that the schools operate on the very same per-student budget as any other comparable school in NYC.

claims.text: Disadvantaged students in the Icahn schools performed better in debate than more advantaged rivals because they avoided 'developmentally appropriate' schooling.
claims.source_passage: Why did these disadvantaged students perform better in debate than their more advantaged rivals? It was because the Core Knowledge students had NOT been schooled under a “developmentally appropriate” mode of schooling, as their rival schools had been.

claims.text: Inducting children into the public sphere early and systematically creates a cumulative effect that cannot be matched by intensive efforts in later grades.
claims.source_passage: When this knowledge is imparted early and systematically a cumulative effect starts taking place, which after a time cannot be matched by a hurry-up intensity in later grades.

claims.text: Developmental, child-centered, and project-based orientations stress individual growth and natural development at the expense of communal knowledge.
claims.source_passage: their young rivals, who were probably schooled under a developmental, child-centered, and project-based orientation that had stressed individual “growth” and “natural development.”

claims.text: The idea that reading comprehension is a general skill is false.
claims.source_passage: the convenient but false idea held by our schools that skills such as reading comprehension are general skills.

claims.text: A reading test is actually a test of background knowledge in disguise.
claims.source_passage: “a reading test is a knowledge test in disguise,” (Willingham), and the knowledge needed is specific to the text being read.

claims.text: Practicing general comprehension skills is a fruitless use of school time for disadvantaged children.
claims.source_passage: The fruitless practicing of general comprehension skills simply wastes school time for disadvantaged children.

claims.text: Disadvantaged children catch up to advantaged children when they are systematically provided with equivalent background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: These children from the South Bronx have caught up because they were systematically gaining more knowledge, and now know as much as the advantaged child.

claims.text: Reading comprehension requires knowledge specific to the text being read rather than general skills.
claims.source_passage: needed is specific to the text being read.

claims.text: High-performing advantaged children achieve success because they spend time gaining knowledge rather than doing comprehension exercises.
claims.source_passage: Did the high-performing advantaged child in Manhattan undergo fruitless comprehension exercises at home? No, she was too busy gaining more knowledge.

claims.text: South Bronx children closed the achievement gap because they systematically gained more knowledge until they reached parity with advantaged peers.
claims.source_passage: These children from the South Bronx have caught up because they were systematically gaining more knowledge, and now know as much as the advantaged child.

claims.text: Core Knowledge is a curriculum guide rather than a charter school management company.
claims.source_passage: Core Knowledge is not a charter company. It’s just a curriculum guide with some freely downloadable curricular materials.

claims.text: Lottery-based studies of oversubscribed charter schools can isolate the effect of curriculum by equalizing the factor of parental solicitude.
claims.source_passage: Since the entire group of kindergartners who applied came from homes with solicitous parents, that factor touted by opponents of charter schools could be eliminated.

claims.text: Low-income students in Denver Core Knowledge schools achieved a verbal effect-size of 1.46 after six years.
claims.source_passage: The latest data show that after six years with the low-income set of matched pairs, the verbal effect-size is 1.46.

claims.text: An effect size of 1.46 is an extraordinary and outlier-level result in educational research.
claims.source_passage: An effect size of 1.4 is not mentioned in the guide. Yet that is what Professor Grissmer found.

claims.text: 92 percent of Core Knowledge low-income children in the Denver study performed better than the average of the matched public-school control group.
claims.source_passage: In the measurement of verbal skill, 92 percent of the Core Knowledge low-income children scored above the average of the matched public-school low-income children.

claims.text: The Denver study results are consistent with forty years of research on the nature of language and early grade topic progression.
claims.source_passage: These results are highly consistent with what we have learned in the past four decades about the nature of language, and the effect of coherent common topic progression in the early grades.

claims.text: Instituting a policy of curricular specificity requires abandoning the dominant educational mindset of developmentalism.
claims.source_passage: To institute that policy will require a change in mindset – away from the principle of developmentalism, the subject of the next chapter.

claims.text: General-purpose reading and thinking skills would be desirable educational goals if they actually existed as real entities.
claims.source_passage: Those would be fine goals if such all-purpose entities existed. But they do not, and any cognitive psychologist who claimed they do would lose reputation and respect from his or her colleagues.

claims.text: Topic-based testing provides a valid measure of school effectiveness in addition to student achievement.
claims.source_passage: Hence, Shanker’s proposal of topic-based tests would gauge both the effectiveness of the school as well as the achievement of the student.

claims.text: Physical inclusivity in schools is potentially harmful if it is not accompanied by linguistic and mental inclusiveness.
claims.source_passage: Yet physical inclusiveness is worthless or even harmful without linguistic inclusiveness.

claims.text: The public must exert direct political pressure on state legislators to force them to abandon scientifically incorrect educational theories.
claims.source_passage: It’s essential that the public understand this scientific truth and exert pressure on our state legislators to abandon evasive scientific mistakes and get to work.

claims.text: Parents and citizens should use their voting power specifically to support legislators who commit to grade-by-grade curriculum specificity.
claims.source_passage: So, my hope is that parents and patriots will start voting for legislators who commit to specific “statewide curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment systems,”

claims.text: The successful implementation of the Shanker principle at schools like Lyles-Crouch has likely caused housing dislocations and increased local property values.
claims.source_passage: And I apologize if the growing reputation of the school has caused any housing dislocations. But if so, the prior homeowners probably got a good price, since obviously some white families have moved into the neighborhood to send their kids to that school.

claims.text: There is no valid technique for teaching students to 'find the main idea' of a text in the absence of relevant background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: There is no reliable technique for “finding the main idea” if, as is now understood in our psychology departments, “a reading test is a knowledge test in disguise,” (Willingham), and the knowledge needed is specific to the text being read.

claims.text: The 100 percent admission rate of Icahn eighth graders to selective high schools is significantly more impressive given the citywide average acceptance rate of only 14 percent.
claims.source_passage: Their 100 percent admission to select high schools is especially striking. In the whole of New York City the rate of acceptance of the 28,000 ambitious eighth graders who take the admission test is 14 percent!

claims.text: The educational establishment frequently rebrands failed theories like constructivism with new names such as 'challenge-based learning' to avoid admitting failure.
claims.source_passage: When an experiment shows that it doesn’t work, we don’t give up the natural-development, learning-by-doing idea, we stop calling it “constructivism” and we call it “discovery learning.” ... Since Kirschner et al. wrote there have arisen new names like “challenge-based learning,” and “activity-based learning.”

claims.text: Legislatures should only mandate which topics are taught and in what sequence, without dictating how publishers or teachers interpret those topics.
claims.source_passage: But we’re only asking our legislatures to decide topic sequences, not how the topics are treated.

evidence.description: Evidence from cognitive psychology regarding the domain-specificity of expertise and the lack of transferable general skills.
evidence.source_reference: Anders Ericsson, Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2020)
evidence.source_passage: The technical literature on skills is clear: being expert in one domain does not make you an expert in another. Expertise is area-specific; skill and expertise are domain specific to use the technical term.

evidence.description: Critique of the first-grade Language Arts framework in the Common Core as an example of unspecific, skill-centric standards.
evidence.source_passage: Describe characters, settings, and major events in a story, using key details... Note the implication of such “standards.” We don’t need to specify a definite shared story for first grade...

evidence.description: Scientific research demonstrating the failure of readability formulas to accurately measure text difficulty.
evidence.source_reference: Bruce, Bertram C.; Rubin, Ann D.; & Starr, Kathleen S. (1981). Why readability formulas fail.
evidence.source_passage: Answer: because measures of “readability” have been proved nonsense since the early 1980s.

evidence.description: The technical literature on expertise confirms that skills are specific to domains of knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: K. Anders Ericsson, et al., Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance, 2020
evidence.source_passage: The technical literature is clear on “the domain-specificity of skills.”

evidence.description: A study identifying why readability formulas (used as a precursor to 'complexity') fail as valid measures.
evidence.source_reference: Bruce, Bertram C.; Rubin, Ann D.; & Starr, Kathleen S. (1981). Why readability formulas fail.
evidence.source_passage: measures of “readability” have been proved nonsense since the early 1980s.

evidence.description: A scientific analysis of why minimal guidance, inquiry-based, and constructivist teaching methods fail.
evidence.source_reference: Kirschner et al., 'Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work'
evidence.source_passage: experimental studies disproving constructivism have employed descriptive titles like... “Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work”

evidence.description: Spanish study showing the positive impact of both specific and general knowledge on future income.
evidence.source_reference: Cristina Bellés-Obrero and Emma Duchini, “Who Benefits from General Knowledge?” Economics of Education Review, 2021
evidence.source_passage: A recent long-term study in Spain showed that higher income of students arises from two principles...

evidence.description: The Cohen curve confirms the relation between teenage verbal tests and adult income.
A long-term study in Spain showed higher student income arises from specific vocational knowledge plus wide general knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Cristina Bellés-Obrero and Emma Duchini, “Who Benefits from General Knowledge?” Economics of Education Review, Vol. 85, Dec. 2021.

evidence.description: Exemplary schools in Alexandria (VA), South Bronx (NYC), and Denver (CO) illustrate the benefits of the Shanker principle via the Core Knowledge sequence.
Demographic and performance data for Lyles-Crouch School provided by Principal Dr. Patricia Zissios, showing 10% ELL, 28% poverty, and scores in the 90th percentile.
Lyles-Crouch history exam results where 99% of students passed.
State and regional awards including Virginia Board of Education’s Award of Excellence and Northern Virginia magazine rankings for Lyles-Crouch.
Verbatim objectives from the Alexandria City Public Schools website for first grade ELA, Math, Science, and Social Studies.
Excerpts from the Core Knowledge Sequence used at Lyles-Crouch, detailing specific poems, stories, sayings, and historical facts for Kindergarten.
evidence.source_passage: These are excerpts from the “curriculum framework,” the Core Knowledge Sequence, that is being followed in Lyles-Crouch

evidence.description: Lyles-Crouch student verbal ability rankings reaching the top in the state of Virginia.
Waitlist and rejection data showing 25,000 students turned away annually from seven specific South Bronx schools.
Citywide debate tournament victory by low-income South Bronx students.
evidence.source_passage: They have just won their age division in New York City in a citywide debate contest.

evidence.description: Comparison of admission rates to selective high schools: 100% for South Bronx CK schools vs. 14% citywide.
The use of a blind lottery for admission to the South Bronx charter schools.
Admission rates for Icahn Core Knowledge 8th graders (100%) versus the general New York City specialized high school admission rate (14%).
Demographic data for seven Icahn Charter schools showing high poverty (up to 85%), high minority populations (90% Black/Latino), and significant percentages of homeless, ELL, and disabled students.
The award of 'Recognition School' status by New York State to six out of seven Icahn schools, a status given to only 13% of schools state-wide.
US Department of Education National Blue Ribbon awards received by four Icahn Schools.
David Grissmer's five-million-dollar study from the Arnold Foundation researching the effectiveness of Core Knowledge.
David Grissmer's matched-pair lottery study of Core Knowledge charter schools in Denver, tracking students for six years.
evidence.source_reference: David Grissmer, Arnold Foundation funded research
evidence.source_passage: Grissmer could therefore study and compare the students who would attend the CK schools and those who would attend the regular public schools, because they lost the blind lottery and were denied admission to the CK schools.

evidence.description: Standardized interpretation of effect sizes (0.1 small, 0.3 medium, 0.5 large, 0.8 very large).
evidence.source_reference: Standard educational research guides (unnamed by Hirsch)
evidence.source_passage: Here’s what the experts say about effect sizes- .10 is a small effect; .30 is a medium effect; .50 is a large effect; .80 is a very large effect.

concepts.author_definition: A 'delusory' educational concept suggesting there are all-purpose reading-comprehension, critical-thinking, or complexity-managing abilities that function independently of specific content knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The mainstream educational belief that learning 'how' to read or think is a transferable skill that can be applied to any content.
concepts.source_passage: And second, it’s a delusion to suppose that there exist all-purpose reading-comprehension skills, and all-purpose critical-thinking skills, and all-purpose complexity-managing skills.

concepts.author_definition: The technical principle that expertise and skills are tied to specific areas of knowledge and do not automatically transfer to different tasks or subjects.
concepts.source_passage: Expertise is area-specific; skill and expertise are domain specific to use the technical term. Acquired human skills are not, by and large, transferable.

concepts.author_definition: A term used in modern standards that the author argues is a scientifically invalid substitute for 'readability,' masking the fact that text difficulty depends on an individual's familiarity with the subject matter.
concepts.common_alternative: A measure of the difficulty of a text based on vocabulary, sentence structure, and conceptual density.
concepts.source_passage: This term has zero scientific validity, because text-complexity is dependent upon familiarity, which varies from person to person.

concepts.author_definition: The educational theory that children learn best by 'constructing' their own knowledge through hands-on projects and minimal teacher guidance; characterized by the author as ineffective.
concepts.common_alternative: Discovery learning, inquiry-based learning, challenge-based learning.

concepts.author_definition: The practice of defining a specific, shared sequence of factual topics to be taught at specific grade levels.
The increased cognitive efficiency resulting from a large store of background knowledge and vocabulary, allowing for faster processing of new information.
A state beyond physical presence where all children in a classroom share the linguistic and mental background needed to understand instruction.
concepts.common_alternative: Physical inclusivity (merely having diverse students in the same room).

concepts.author_definition: The specific communal background knowledge and vocabulary that all third graders in a system must share to communicate effectively with each other and their teachers.
Vague, skill-based standards (e.g., 'Gaining Meaning from Informational Text') that do not specify the actual content knowledge students must acquire.
A specific, detailed sequence of topics that ensures transparency regarding what students are responsible for learning.
The practice of selecting only the most advantaged or capable students for a school to inflate performance results.
The mastery of public language, including its publicly shared associations and values, which allows for effective communication and participation in the public sphere.
concepts.common_alternative: Commonly refers to biological ancestry or cultural heritage rather than linguistic and informational commonality.

concepts.author_definition: A shared communicative tool required for public language-events (like debate) that relies on specific background knowledge shared across the group.
The educational and communicative gap experienced by children from low-income families that a communal curriculum is designed to bridge.
A pedagogical orientation focusing on 'natural development,' child-centered learning, and project-based work, which Hirsch argues neglects the systematic building of communal knowledge.
The degree to which parents are helpful, involved, and motivated to seek out better educational opportunities for their children.
concepts.common_alternative: Often discussed as 'parental involvement' or 'socio-economic status indicators.'
concepts.source_passage: the parental solicitude-quotient would be equivalent in both groups.

concepts.author_definition: A quantitative measure of the magnitude of an experimental effect, used to compare the strength of different educational interventions.
The communicative commonality and shared knowledge required for individuals (both children and adults) to function effectively within a community or nation.
A prevalent rhetorical tactic in educational teacher-training where failed instructional methods are given new titles rather than being abandoned.
The increased ability to learn new things rapidly and understand one's environment more effectively resulting from a wide vocabulary and background knowledge.
The systematic process of early schooling that prepares children to engage in the shared domain of commerce, newspapers, and democratic decision-making.
The use of scientifically invalid but sophisticated-sounding terms (like 'complexity') to replace discredited terms and avoid curriculum specificity.
A high standard of research quality and exactness, as exemplified by the work of David Grissmer.

cases.name: Common Core State Standards (First-Grade Language Arts)
cases.description: The author analyzes the specific requirements for first-graders in the Common Core, such as 'Describe characters' or 'Identify words that suggest feelings,' to argue that they avoid naming specific content (like specific stories), relying instead on the false hope that kids will develop general skills through any text.
key_passages: Take for example the current Common Core State Standards. They are good examples of the evasiveness and fear of the inevitable criticism that would certainly come initially with the specificity that Shanker calls for.

Note the implication of such “standards.” We don’t need to specify a definite shared story for first grade, because (so the standards imply) once you “identify words that suggest feelings or appeal to the senses,” then you will be able to identify a whole range of words...
contestable_aspects: The author's characterization of the standards as 'evasive' and 'fearful' of criticism.
The claim that the standards imply content knowledge is irrelevant.

cases.name: Spain (Bellés-Obrero & Duchini Study)
cases.description: A long-term economic study in Spain investigating which factors contribute to higher income for students. It found that a combination of specific early vocational knowledge and broad general knowledge was key.
key_passages: A recent long-term study in Spain showed that higher income of students arises from two principles: the more specific knowledge you learn early about a specific vocation plus the wide general knowledge

cases.name: American Elementary Education
cases.description: The current state of US schooling, characterized by the author as failing due to adherence to 'natural-development' theories and 'general skills' curriculum models.
key_passages: American elementary education is thus based currently upon two incorrect theories: namely that project-based learning is effective, and that general skills of reading comprehension and critical-thinking exist.

cases.name: Spain Income Study
cases.description: A long-term study examining the sources of student income levels, concluding that success is predicated on both specific vocational knowledge and wide general knowledge for communication.
key_passages: A recent long-term study in Spain showed that higher income of students arises from two principles: the more specific knowledge you learn early about a specific vocation plus the wide general knowledge required to communicate well.

cases.name: Core Knowledge Foundation sequence
cases.description: A grade-by-grade topic sequence designed by a non-profit foundation to provide the curricular specificity the author argues is necessary for educational equality. Used as a proxy for the 'Shanker Principle' in the absence of a state-mandated specific curriculum.
key_passages: I’ll analyze the results of schools using the topic sequence designed by the Core Knowledge Foundation. Whatever its defects, it offers the specificity needed to exemplify the benefits of the Shanker principle.

cases.name: Lyles-Crouch School
cases.description: A regular public school in Alexandria, VA, formerly segregated, now highly diverse with a 20% mobility rate. Under Dr. Patricia Zissios, it implemented the Core Knowledge Sequence and rose to the top 5% of schools in Virginia, outperforming district peers despite having a challenging student body.
key_passages: The Lyles-Crouch regular public school in Alexandria, Virginia used used to be an all-black public school before desegregation.
Today, we have 441 students, 10% ELL [English Language Learners] (mainly Ethiopians speaking Amharic), 10% special education (with citywide autism class), and 28% living in poverty.
Our current scores put us in the top 5% of the entire Commonwealth of Virginia for academic achievement.

cases.name: Alexandria Framework
cases.description: An example of a current educational framework that the author argues lacks the specificity needed to give disadvantaged students a fair chance at succeeding on standardized tests.
key_passages: Does the Alexandria framework offer schools and teachers enough guidance to prepare disadvantaged students to have a fair shot at the upcoming test? No!
contestable_aspects: Whether the framework actually lacks guidance or if the guidance provided is intentionally flexible to allow for professional teacher judgment.

cases.name: Lyles-Crouch Traditional Academy
cases.description: A public school that implemented a highly specific Core Knowledge curriculum including fine arts and music lists. The author credits this specific content for the school's high state rankings in verbal ability.
key_passages: Whatever objections might be raised against this curriculum framework, it’s obvious that it has raised the verbal abilities of its students to the top in its state.

cases.name: South Bronx Core Knowledge Schools
cases.description: A group of charter schools (including Success Academies and Classical Charter Schools) in the poorest area of NYC that use the Core Knowledge curriculum. Despite serving low-income populations, they achieve 100% selective high school admission and win citywide academic contests.
key_passages: All the schools in the South Bronx could be doing this kind of thing! And several charters are doing so, like the Success Academies and the Classical Charter Schools in the Bronx.
Every single eighth grader in all seven of the South Bronx Core Knowledge schools proceeded to win admission to a selective high school!
contestable_aspects: Whether the success is due to curriculum or other charter school factors (longer hours, teacher selection, etc.)

cases.name: Icahn Core Knowledge Charter Schools
cases.description: Seven K-8 charter schools in the South Bronx serving a demographic of approximately 75% poverty and 90% minority students. Despite these disadvantages, 100% of their 8th graders won admission to selective high schools, and the schools have consistently received state and federal recognition for high achievement using the Core Knowledge curriculum.
key_passages: Every single eighth grader in all seven of the South Bronx Core Knowledge schools proceeded to win admission to a selective high school!
In the whole of New York City the rate of acceptance of the 28,000 ambitious eighth graders who take the admission test is 14 percent!
Note that about 75 percent of them are in poverty; 90 percent are black or Latino; 6 percent are homeless; 4 percent are English language learners, and 8 percent are disabled.

cases.name: Denver, Colorado Core Knowledge Research
cases.description: A large-scale study conducted by David Grissmer funded by the Arnold Foundation to determine if the Core Knowledge curriculum specifically improves educational outcomes.
key_passages: The reason for my third and last example is its scientific punctilio.
He secured five million dollars from the Arnold Foundation to research two questions, whether in fact charter schools worked, and whether Core Knowledge in particular worked as a curriculum.

cases.name: Denver, Colorado Core Knowledge Lotteries
cases.description: A study conducted by David Grissmer utilizing the oversubscription of Core Knowledge charter schools in Denver. Because admission was based on a blind lottery, Grissmer could compare two groups of children with equally motivated parents (those who won the lottery and those who lost), effectively neutralizing the variable of 'parental solicitude' that critics usually use to explain charter school success. After six years, low-income students in the CK schools showed a verbal effect size of 1.46.
key_passages: There are a lot of CK charters in the Denver area. But they are oversubscribed, so the acceptance rate is limited. That presents a research opportunity using matched pairs of kindergartners all of whom applied to the CK schools.
The latest data show that after six years with the low-income set of matched pairs, the verbal effect-size is 1.46.
contestable_aspects: Small sample size for low-income subgroups
Generalizability of results from charter lottery applicants to the general population

thinkers.name: Albert Shanker
thinkers.key_idea: The necessity of statewide curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment systems to ensure students and teachers know what is being tested and taught.
thinkers.engagement: The author bases the entire chapter (and the 'Shanker Principle') on Shanker's quote, praising him as 'brilliant and brave' and the only official with the courage to call for curriculum specificity.
key_passages: What we really need—at the very least—are statewide curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment systems.
The brilliant and brave Al Shanker... was, I believe, the only person in an official job in the modern era of American education who has had the courage to publish such sentiments.

thinkers.name: K. Anders Ericsson
thinkers.key_idea: Skill and expertise are domain-specific and do not transfer across different areas of knowledge.
thinkers.source_work: Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2020)
thinkers.engagement: The author uses Ericsson's research on expertise to provide the 'technical reason' why Shanker's content-based curriculum is necessary and why the 'general skills' approach is a delusion.
key_passages: The technical literature on skills is clear: being expert in one domain does not make you an expert in another. Expertise is area-specific; skill and expertise are domain specific...

thinkers.name: Al Shanker
thinkers.key_idea: The need for a specific statewide curriculum framework to ensure students learn concrete content.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch views Shanker as a 'brave and insightful' leader whose principles regarding curriculum specificity should be reclaimed by current authorities.
key_passages: That’s because no one in current authority is as brave and insightful as the late, much-missed Al Shanker.
note Shanker’s answer to the question “Who decides statewide curriculum frameworks?” Under the Constitution the states do.

thinkers.name: Kirschner, Sweller, and Clark
thinkers.key_idea: Minimal guidance during instruction (constructivism) is a failure based on how human cognition works.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their work as definitive scientific proof that the developmental/project-based methods dominant in teacher colleges are ineffective.
key_passages: experimental studies disproving constructivism have employed descriptive titles like the following one by Kirschner et al. in refereed scientific journals: “Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work”

thinkers.name: Jeffrey Litt
thinkers.key_idea: The logic of the language/shared-background-knowledge argument is the key to educational success for disadvantaged students.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch praises Litt as a 'self-effacing genius' who successfully implemented Core Knowledge theory to achieve unprecedented results in the South Bronx.
key_passages: the superintendent of the seven schools, Jeff Litt, is a gentle, self-effacing genius who years ago grasped the logic of the language/shared-background-knowledge argument.

thinkers.name: Daniel Willingham
thinkers.key_idea: A reading test is a knowledge test in disguise.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Willingham's psychological insight to debunk the idea of reading as a general skill.
key_passages: “a reading test is a knowledge test in disguise,” (Willingham)

thinkers.name: David Grissmer
thinkers.key_idea: Educational research must meet a standard of 'scientific punctilio' and 'unchallengeable quality' to prove what works.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Grissmer as an authority whose large-scale funded research provides scientific validation for the Core Knowledge curriculum.
key_passages: David Grissmer is an educational researcher well-known for the unchallengeable quality of his research.

actors.name: Albert Shanker
actors.context: Former head of the American Federation of Teachers.

actors.name: American Federation of Teachers (AFT)
actors.context: The organization Shanker led while advocating for curriculum frameworks.

actors.name: Common Core State Standards
actors.context: The current educational framework used as an example of vague, skills-based standards.

actors.name: State Legislatures
actors.context: Accused of abandoning their responsibility to set specific curriculum content in favor of vague standards and avoiding 'contentious' decisions.

actors.name: Teacher-training faculties
actors.context: Described as being under the power of the 'developmental-project-method tradition' and continuing to promote ineffective methods under new names.

actors.name: Common Core Standards
actors.context: Used as an example of vague standards that use unscientific terms like 'complexity' to avoid curriculum specificity.

actors.name: Core Knowledge Foundation
actors.context: Non-profit organization that created the specific topic sequence used as an exemplar in the text.

actors.name: University of Virginia education school
actors.context: Source of warnings to the author regarding the 'cancel culture' of developmentalists.

actors.name: Amplify
actors.context: For-profit publisher that sells a version of the Core Knowledge Language Arts program.

actors.name: Dr. Patricia Zissios
actors.context: Principal of Lyles-Crouch School who provided data on its success.

actors.name: University of Virginia education school
actors.context: Colleagues of Hirsch who warned him about criticisms on social media.

actors.name: Alexandria City Public Schools
actors.context: District used as an example of 'nebulous' and 'non-specific' curricular objectives.

actors.name: Al Shanker
actors.context: Former head of the American Federation of Teachers and proponent of national standards/curriculum.

actors.name: Core Knowledge Foundation
actors.context: The organization that developed the specific curriculum sequence Hirsch advocates for.

actors.name: Amplify
actors.context: A for-profit publisher that sells the Core Knowledge Language Arts curriculum.

actors.name: Albert Shanker
actors.context: His 'principle' of curriculum specificity is the namesake of the chapter and the theoretical basis for the examples provided.

actors.name: Success Academies
actors.context: Charter schools in the South Bronx cited as successful implementers of the Shanker Principle.

actors.name: Classical Charter Schools
actors.context: Cited alongside Success Academies as examples of effective Core Knowledge implementation in the Bronx.

actors.name: American Debate League
actors.context: Organization that ran the tournament won by the South Bronx students.

actors.name: Jeffrey Litt
actors.context: Superintendent of the Icahn Core Knowledge Charter Schools who implemented the successful curriculum.

actors.name: Carl Icahn
actors.context: Financier who donated buildings for several of the schools.

actors.name: New York City Department of Education
actors.context: Governing body for NYC schools; provides some buildings used by Icahn charters.

actors.name: New York State Department of Education
actors.context: Awarded 'Recognition School' status to the Icahn schools.

actors.name: Arnold Foundation
actors.context: Funder of David Grissmer's $5 million research project into charter schools and Core Knowledge.

actors.name: Amplify
actors.context: For-profit publisher of Core Knowledge materials mentioned in endnote 20.

objections_raised.objection: Reading skills can be taught through general exercises like 'identifying words that suggest feelings' using any text, which then transfers to all other texts.
objections_raised.response: This is factually incorrect because expertise is domain-specific; being a good reader of a first-grade book does not automatically make one a good reader of an eighth-grade book without the requisite knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: Text complexity is a valid scientific measure for determining grade-level appropriateness.
objections_raised.response: The term 'complexity' has zero scientific validity because it is dependent on an individual's familiarity with the topic, and its predecessor, 'readability,' was proven nonsensical in the 1980s.

objections_raised.objection: Who decides what children shall learn? Government deciding content is the road to tyranny and what dictatorships do.
objections_raised.response: States have the constitutional responsibility to decide. Legislatures should decide the 'topic sequences' (the facts to be learned) but not the 'how' or the interpretive treatment (e.g., whether Jefferson was a hero or hypocrite).

objections_raised.objection: Teaching specific facts and content is less important than teaching children 'how to learn' or general skills.
objections_raised.response: Skills are domain-specific; you cannot have the skill without the knowledge. General skills of reading comprehension and critical thinking do not exist in isolation.

objections_raised.objection: Hirsch is just trying to sell a particular language arts program (Core Knowledge) for profit.
objections_raised.response: The claim is false 'root and branch.' The Core Knowledge Foundation is a non-profit where no individuals receive royalties, and the materials are available for free as open educational resources.

objections_raised.objection: Successful school examples are often 'creamed' and do not represent the challenges of regular public schools.
objections_raised.source: Participants in the charter school debate
objections_raised.response: Lyles-Crouch is a 'plain vanilla' regular public school with typical problems of poverty, ELL students, and high mobility.

objections_raised.objection: Is the Core Knowledge Sequence exclusionary or narrowly focused on Western traditions?
objections_raised.response: Hirsch provides a list showing diverse sources including Langston Hughes, African folktales, Japanese folktales (Momotaro), and Gwendolyn Brooks, suggesting the sequence is inclusive of various cultures and American backgrounds.

objections_raised.objection: The curriculum framework (specifically the lists of art, music, and historical figures) may be overly prescriptive or flawed.
objections_raised.response: Regardless of theoretical objections, the empirical results (top state scores) prove its effectiveness.

objections_raised.objection: Charter schools 'cream' or select only the top-performing students to achieve high results.
objections_raised.response: New York City mandates that all charters accept students via blind lottery, and the Icahn schools follow this, admitting students as kindergartners through a lottery where 'nobody leaves.'

objections_raised.objection: Charter schools only succeed because they attract students from more solicitous, helpful, and motivated parents.
objections_raised.response: The Grissmer lottery study eliminates this factor because both the control group (lottery losers) and the experimental group (lottery winners) came from homes with parents motivated enough to apply to the school.

The Cohen curve (reproduced in the preface)

how can its public schools avoid the incoherence that will be described in the fourth chapter by two experienced teachers?
The next chapter devoted to “developmentalism” explains why this dominant view has been a chief cause of our decline in unity and reading ability.
The next chapter’ devoted to “developmentalism” explains why this dominant view has been a chief cause of our decline in unity and reading ability.
The next chapter’ devoted to “developmentalism” explains why this dominant view has been a chief cause of our decline in unity and reading ability.
the principle of developmentalism, the subject of the next chapter.
